title: Bouquet combing pattern
subtitle: Building the classic bouquet from a nonpareil base with a vertical half-comb stroke
excerpt: The bouquet is one of the most recognisable marbling patterns: tight curved arcs that resemble petals or fans, arranged in repeating columns across the sheet. It is produced in two stages: first, a nonpareil combed base (narrow parallel chevrons), then a single pass of a half-spacing comb down the columns to gather the chevrons into bouquet curves.
sourceNotes: Charles Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853), chapter on combed patterns including the bouquet. Franz Weisse, Das Buntpapier (1892), documents the chevron-then-bouquet sequence. Both pre-1928. The pattern name and sequence are standard in Victorian and Edwardian marbling manuals.

content.text: is built on top of a
base. The nonpareil provides the fine chevron grid; a single pass of a

down the length of the tray gathers every other chevron column into a curved petal arc. The resulting pattern has columns of fan shapes alternating with straight chevron columns, which together create the bouquet effect. Mastering this pattern requires understanding the nonpareil tutorial first.

items.name: Marbling paints, 3 to 5 colours, prepared and tested for spreading
Fine comb (tooth spacing 5 to 8 mm) for the nonpareil base
Wide comb (tooth spacing 10 to 16 mm) for the bouquet pass, exactly double the fine comb spacing

content.text: Skim the size surface. Drop the first colour in horizontal rows across the full tray surface. Drop the second colour between the first-colour rows. Repeat for all colours until the surface is covered in even horizontal stripes.
Draw the fine comb across the width of the tray (left to right), teeth just below the surface. This divides the horizontal stripes into a chevron grid.
Draw the fine comb down the length of the tray (near to far), teeth just below the surface. This produces the nonpareil: a fine interlocking chevron pattern across the whole surface.
Position the wide comb at the near end of the tray so the teeth align between the fine comb rows, not on top of them. The wide comb has exactly half the teeth of the fine comb.

Draw the wide comb slowly and smoothly down the length of the tray in a single steady stroke. Each tooth gathers and curves the chevrons in its path into a fan arc. Do not change speed mid-stroke; irregular speed produces uneven arcs.
Lay the alum-coated paper on the surface, rinse, and dry as in the stone pattern tutorial.

items.fix: Check comb alignment by holding it above the tray before inserting. Slow the stroke and keep it completely even.
items.cause: Wide comb teeth not positioned between the nonpareil rows, or comb drawn at uneven speed.
items.symptom: Bouquet arcs are irregular and do not form cleanly

items.fix: Use 4 colours maximum for the first attempts. Allow each colour drop to fully spread before dropping the next.
items.cause: Too many colours used or colours dropped too close together, merging before combing.
items.symptom: Nonpareil base looks muddied before the bouquet pass
